evaluate and optimize neuromusculoskeletal conditions If any of the procedures in this course are outside your scope of practice, that material will be for informational purposes. Please refer to those who can perform these procedures.

your responsibility to investigate the laws of your area of practice to know if it is 
legal for you to practice what you learned in any QAT class or from any teaching 
materials. 2

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Objectives of the QAT Home Study Course By the end of this course, the student should be able to: 1. Perform 50 neurological muscle testings for more than 50 muscles. 2. Locate the 24 acupuncture points used in the QAT evaluating and balancing 
acupuncture meridian pairs. 3. Perform a self-treatment for abnormal switching. 4. Perform a self-treatment for polarity issues. 5. Perform a self-treatment with TNT for cranial bones, teeth, breasts, and other 
bones and joints of the body. 6. Perform a self-treatment with TNT for muscles. 7. Perform a self-treatment with TNT for ligaments, tendons, and scar tissue. 8. Perform a self-treatment with TNT for organs and glands. 9.. Perform a self-treatment with CTB  for cranial bones, teeth, breast, and other 
bones, joints, and the body. 10. Perform a self-treatment with CTB for muscles. 11. Perform a self-treatment with CTB for ligaments, tendons, and scar tissue. 12. Perform a self-treatment with CTB for organs and glands. 13. Perform a self-treatment with a quantum device for inflamed tissues. 14. Perform a self-treatment with quantum devices for testing and treating 
acupuncture meridians and points. 15. Perform a self-treatment with quantum devices for balancing various pairs 
of brain lobes, nerves, muscles, and ligaments. 5
section: 13. Perform a self-treatment with a quantum device for inflamed tissues.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Manual Muscle Testing Basics In the live course, you became familiar with the concept of testing muscles for 
use as an indicator muscle. We used a very light force over 1.5 seconds. Now, we 
are going to test each muscle to see if it can fulfill its function to enable the client 
to walk, lift, pull, and move in all ways in their life. If a muscle cannot turn on very 
well or not at all, the body will experience pain and weakness. Movements will 
be unbalanced, leading to joint dysfunction and kinetic chain disruption. When 
muscles are not doing their assigned duties, other muscles will have to be 
recruited. The person will experience increased accidents, causing ligament 
strains, falls, and increased fatigue. Therefore, it is incumbent on the therapist to 
do these assessments and corrections periodically for each client. Have good anatomy and kinesiology textbooks handy if you need to become 
more familiar with the locations of the muscles and what movements the muscles 
do. The accompanying video, written descriptions, and photographs should 
greatly assist the learning process. In this type of muscle testing, the basic process 
mainly involves the therapist placing the target muscle in a maximum passive 
contracted state (i.e., bring the two ends of the muscles together as close as 
comfortably possible and perform a moderate force for several seconds to cause 
the target muscle to contract briefly. Note that some of the positioning may not 
be obvious. We want to isolate each muscle without activating other muscles. That sometimes causes us to position the joint differently than expected. We want to know whether or not the client’s nervous system can recognize a 
challenge to a muscle and lock it into position. We are not asking the client to 
contract the muscle maximally. We want the client to meet our moderate force 
for a few seconds. This is in contrast to testing a muscle for use as an indicator 
muscle. Either the muscle locks into position and holds, or it will not be able to do that 
and allows the muscle to unlock, either gradually or immediately. The 
terminology commonly used in applied kinesiology is a strong versus a weak 
muscle. That can be misleading. It is a matter of whether or not the muscle can 
neurologically turn on and stay on during the challenge. 6
section: 13. Perform a self-treatment with a quantum device for inflamed tissues.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC In this home study module, we will take the time to fully explain how to do 
manual muscle testing for most of the major muscles of the body. This testing is 
quite educational for those clients who need more definitive proof that the QAT 
procedures are accomplishing some benefit for the client. It is hard for the client 
to deny the QAT process is of value when, in one moment, a particular muscle 
was not able to lock when given a brief and moderate challenge, and in the next 
moment, it can. In the live class, we tested the muscles using indicator muscles. In this home study 
course, you will learn how to directly challenge each muscle to determine if it is 
less than optimal. 7
section: 13. Perform a self-treatment with a quantum device for inflamed tissues.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Major Points For Muscle Testing Both the client and the therapist should drink some water, perhaps 1 cup, before 
starting a session. The client should remove all jewelry, watches, and energetic items. A locked muscle feels very solidly locked into position during the testing. Any drifting, shaking erratically, or sponginess response is considered an 
unlocked response. Instruct the client to keep their teeth slightly apart during the testing. Instruct the client not to hold their breath during the testing. A test will be invalid if the client moves their body around to recruit other muscles 
during the testing. The therapist should not clench their teeth, close their eyes, or hold their breath 
during the testing. The therapist should not squeeze the client’s body part during the testing. We 
want to avoid creating discomfort for the client, which may interfere with the 
reflex testing. The muscle should lock within 2 inches of movement. The client’s hands and feet remain uncrossed during testing unless that is part of 
the testing position. The client must be engaged in the process. If they space out or are distracted by 
a thought during the testing, the result will be invalid. With some clients, you 
must keep them in the present moment by verbally engaging with them. Use the muscle testing chart located in this manual for your convenience. Follow along with the accompanying videos practice with your friends and family 
until you have mastered all of the positions. Important note: the pictures on the right side of each muscle test depict what the 
body would look like if the therapist continued to push, pull, or rotate far past 
the actual range of motion required to see if the client’s muscles will lock or not. 8
section: 13. Perform a self-treatment with a quantum device for inflamed tissues.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Supine Positions 1. Anterior neck flexors The client brings their chin to their chest. The therapist has one hand on the back 
of the head to partially offset the weight of the head. The other hand is on the 
forehead. The line of drive is to bring the head towards the table. Do this in 
neutral, 10° (not pictured), and 45° on both sides. 0° 45° 11

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 2. Levator scapula The client moves their shoulder towards the hip on the same side. Their arm is 
tucked against the lateral side of their body. While keeping the shoulder 
depressed, the therapist’s line of drive is to push the arm away from the side of 
the body. 3. Upper trapezius The client brings their ear and shoulder together as much as comfortably possible. The therapist’s action is to pry the neck and shoulder apart. 12

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 4. Middle trapezius The client moves their upper extremity perpendicular to the lateral side of the 
trunk and keeps their elbow locked straight. The therapist pulls the wrist up 
towards the ceiling. 5. Lower trapezius With the elbow locked straight, position the client’s upper extremity 45° away 
from the lateral trunk. The therapist pulls on the forearm near the wrist in the 
direction of their opposite shoulder. 13

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 6. Supraspinatus With the elbow locked straight, the client holds the arm up about 30° and slightly 
out to the side. The arm should be rotated so that the palm faces the private 
parts. 7. Anterior deltoid Position the client’s straight arm a little less than 45° in front of the body with the 
palm facing down, lateral to the thigh. The therapist presses down on the 
forearm near the wrist. The client is instructed to keep their shoulders level 
during the test. 14

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 8. Middle deltoid The client’s arm is perpendicular to the lateral side of the trunk, and the elbow is 
at 90°. The therapist’s action is to push the elbow towards the lateral side of the 
body. 9. Posterior deltoid The client’s arm is perpendicular to the lateral side of the trunk, and the elbow is 
at 90°, with the arm partially internally rotated. The therapist’s action is to push 
the elbow towards the lateral side of the body with a bit of added flexion of the 
shoulder. 9. Pectoralis major clavicular 15

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 10. Pectoralis major clavicular The client’s upper extremity is perpendicular to the anterior trunk. Their forearm 
is rotated, so the thumb points towards their feet. The therapist’s action is to 
push the upper extremity down and away from the lateral side of the body. 11. Pectoralis major sternal The client’s upper extremity is perpendicular to the anterior trunk. Their forearm 
is rotated, so the thumb points towards their feet. The therapist’s action is to 
push the upper extremity up and away from the lateral side of the body. 16

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 12. Subscapularis The client’s arm is perpendicular to the lateral trunk. The elbow is at 90°, and the 
forearm and palm lie on the table. The therapist’s action is to pull the forearm up 
from the table. 13. Infraspinatus The client’s arm is perpendicular to the lateral trunk. With the elbow at 90°, the 
shoulder is externally rotated with the back of the hand facing the table. The 
therapist’s line of drive is to rotate the arm so that the fingers point toward the 
ceiling. 17

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 14. Teres minor The client’s arm lies a few inches away from their lateral side. The elbow is at 90°, 
and the palm faces the abdomen. The therapist’s action is to pull the forearm and 
hand toward the abdomen. 15. Teres Major After moving their shoulder to the edge of the table, the client bends their elbow 
and places the back of their hand into the lumbar area of their back, with the 
palm facing the table. Their elbow hangs below the table. The therapist’s action 
is to pull the elbow up toward the ceiling. 18

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 16. Supinator The client moves their shoulder off the edge of the table so the upper extremity 
can be extended. With the elbow locked straight, the forearm is rotated into the 
supinated position. The therapist attempts to rotate the forearm into the 
pronated position. 17. Latissimus dorsi The client’s upper extremity is against the lateral trunk. Keeping the elbow 
straight, the forearm is rotated so the back of the wrist is against the hip. The 
therapist’s line of drive is to move the wrist away from the hip and slightly up off 
the table. 19

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 18. Anterior serratus With the elbow locked straight, position the client’s upper extremity 
perpendicular to the trunk. The forearm is in a neutral position, with the back of 
their shoulder lifted up from the table. The therapist presses on the forearm near 
the wrist in the direction of their feet. 19. Rhomboids The client’s arm lies against their lateral side with the elbow at 90°. The therapist 
moves the elbow away from the side of the body. 20

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 20. Coracobrachialis With the palm facing the table and lateral to the client’s eyes, the elbow is 
positioned 45° up and away (45° shoulder flexion and 45° shoulder abduction). The therapist brings the elbow towards the body’s midline and below the breast. 21. Biceps The client’s arm lies against the lateral side of the body with the palm facing the 
shoulder and elbow flexed at 75°. The therapist attempts to extend the elbow. 21

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 22. Brachioradialis The client’s arm lies against the lateral side of their trunk with the elbow at 75° 
flexion. The forearm is neutral (thumb pointing towards the shoulder). The 
therapist attempts to straighten the elbow. 23. Triceps The client’s upper extremity lies against their lateral trunk with the palm facing 
up and the elbow at 160° flexion. The therapist attempts to flex the elbow. 22

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 24. Pronator teres The client’s arm lies against the lateral side of the trunk with the elbow flexed at 
60°. Their forearm is rotated into full pronation. The therapist attempts to rotate 
the forearm into supination. 25. Pronator quadratus With the elbow fully flexed, the client’s arm lies against the lateral side of the 
trunk. The forearm is rotated into full pronation. The therapist attempts to rotate 
the forearm into supination. 23

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 26. Opponens pollicis The client places the tips of their thumb and pinky finger together to form the 
shape of a circle. The therapist uses their pinky fingers to try to pry apart the two 
fingers of the client. 27. Respiratory diaphragm The client places the tips of their fingers (or the blue pad) below the cleavage of 
the breast on one side. Using any indicator muscle (in this case, anterior deltoid), 
the therapist activates the IM. If the IM unlocks, the respiratory diaphragm on 
that side will need correction. 24

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 28. Pelvic floor muscles (Pelvic Diaphragm) The client places the tips of their fingers (or the blue pad) superior to the inguinal 
ligament and inferior to the belt line. Using any indicator muscle, the therapist 
activates the IM. If the IM unlocks, the pelvic diaphragm on that side will need 
correction. 29. Quadratus lumborum The therapist positions the lower extremities away from the midline of the body 
and braces the hip. Then, the therapist attempts to bring the lower extremities 
back to the midline. 25

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 30. Gluteus medius The therapist brings one of the lower extremities away from the midline of the 
body. While bracing on the opposite knee, the therapist attempts to bring the 
lower extremity back toward the midline. 31. Tensor fascia lata With the knee locked straight, the therapist internally rotates the lower 
extremity, bringing it 45° up and away from the midline. While bracing the 
opposite thigh, the therapist attempts to bring the ankle towards the other ankle. 26

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 32. Adductors With the knees locked straight and thighs together, the therapist attempts to pull 
one of the lower extremities away from the midline while bracing the opposite 
limb. Be careful not to squeeze! 33. Gracilis With the knee bent a little and locked in that position, the heel of one leg lies 
over the top of the opposing ankle. The therapist attempts to move the heel away 
from the midline while bracing the opposing ankle. Be careful not to squeeze! 27

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 34. Psoas With the client’s knee locked straight, the therapist brings the lower extremity up 
and out 45°. The lower extremity is also rotated externally. From that position, 
the therapist braces the opposite hip while moving the lower extremity down and 
out. 35. Piriformis Position the client’s lower extremity so that the knee is bent at 90° and the hip 
rotated externally (ankle is over the opposite knee). The therapist attempts to 
internally rotate the hip (moving the ankle away from the opposite knee). 28

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 36. Quadriceps Position the client’s lower extremity so that the knee is bent 90° and the hip is 
slightly more than 90°. The therapist places a hand on the top of the thigh near 
the knee and the other hand under the leg near the ankle. The therapist pushes 
on the thigh to flex the hip. 37. Sartorius The therapist positions the heel of one leg over the knee of the opposite leg. The 
therapist then attempts to straighten the same side knee and bring the medial 
ankles together. 29

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 38. Popliteus The therapist bends the client’s knee slightly and drops it outward. The therapist 
has a hand on the outside of the knees and one around the heel. The therapist 
attempts to straighten the knee. 39. Peroneals The client brings the foot up and out (ankle dorsiflexed and everted). The 
therapist anchors the heel while attempting to move the ankle and outer part of 
the foot down and in. 30

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 40. Anterior tibialis The client brings their foot towards the shin (ankle dorsiflexion). The therapist 
attempts to bring their foot away from the shin (ankle plantar flexion). 41. Posterior tibialis The client brings the medial foot down and in. The therapist anchors the heel 
while attempting to move the medial foot and great toe up into a neutral 
position. 31

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 42. Posterior neck extensors The client is sitting, facing the end of the table with knees bent and arms crossed 
on their chest. Their neck is fully extended. With the palm of one hand bracing on 
the sternum and the other hand on the back of their head, the therapist attempts 
to flex the neck. Then with the neck extended and rotated 45°, the therapist 
attempts to flex the neck. Do this in neutral and 45° on both sides. 0° 45° 32

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 43. Rectus abdominis The client sits (not completely upright) with the knees bent and forearms crossed 
over the chest. The therapist holds the knees in place while attempting to push 
the client down on the table. 44. Transverse Abdominis/Obliques – Part 1 The client sits (not completely upright) with the knees bent and forearms crossed 
over the chest. The therapist rotates the trunk 25° for the starting position. The 
therapist attempts to rotate the trunk back to the neutral position. 33

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Transverse Abdominis/Obliques – Part 2 The client sits (not completely upright) with the knees bent and forearms crossed 
over the chest. The therapist positions the trunk to lean a little laterally towards 
the table for the starting position. The therapist attempts to move the side with 
the lower shoulder in the opposite direction. 45. Sacrospinalis The client is prone and lifts one shoulder with their neck extended and rotated 
towards the same side. The therapist has one hand on the sacrum and the other 
hand on the shoulder. The action of the therapist is to push the shoulder towards 
the table. 34

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 46. Gluteus maximus With the knee bent at 90°, the client lifts their knee from the table. The therapist 
stabilizes the leg with one hand around the ankle and pushes on the back of the 
thigh near the knee to bring the knee toward the table. 47. Iliacus Position the client’s lower extremity so that the knee is bent 90° and the foot 
moved laterally away from the midline (internal hip rotation). The therapist 
moves the foot toward the midline (external hip rotation) 35

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 48. Hamstrings Position the client’s lower extremity so the knee is bent at 90°. The therapist has 
one hand stabilizing the anterior knee while the other hand is behind the ankle. The therapist attempts to straighten the knee. 49. Soleus With the knee bent at 90°, the client points and locks their toes towards the 
ceiling (ankle plantar flexion). The therapist stabilizes the back of their thighs 
while pressing with their other hand on the balls of the foot. The therapist 
attempts to straighten the knee. 36

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC 50. Gastrocnemius The ankle is plantarflexed, with the knee bent enough to bring the top of the foot 
a few inches away from the table. The therapist stabilizes the back of the thigh 
while pushing on the balls of the foot to bring the top of the foot towards the 
table. 37

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC How to Do a Complete QAT Treatment on Yourself Once you have completed the live QAT course, you have the fundamentals of 
treating yourself. The only difference between treating others and yourself will 
be that you will not be doing any evaluations or testing on yourself. You will 
only be doing the treatments. Therefore, you will need to assume that 
everything needs treatment. The only other challenge is that you must be 
creative in positioning. Because you are only treating and not testing, you will not need to place an 
entangled device on your CV. Follow along with the video. You will need a few items. 1. Three entangled QAT devices (either the blue pads or the clips), the green 
squares, and the red squares. It will be handy to have the cheat sheet for the 
acupuncture meridian treatment points. 2. One spray bottle of the infused salt solution 3. A yardstick or something like a Thera Cane® with some masking tape so that 
you can treat all of your back. 4. For the TNT, you can use two elastic hair bands to vertically anchor the blue 
pads to the distal leg. For the CTB, you may wear headphones or use a hair band 
to keep the pad on the top of the head (aligned between the ears) while 
treating for the connection to the brain. Regularly do the neurological disorganization procedures if you are around 
emotionally distraught people in your practice or other areas of your life. Please 
note that you will not have to do TNT daily after doing it a few times. Do TNT 
when you have trauma. If you have chronic areas of pain, it is helpful to do QAT 
often to reduce or eliminate the pain and perhaps help the body start to heal 
the area. Your road to health lies in your hands, depending on how much effort 
you want to put into it. 38
section: 2. One spray bottle of the infused salt solution

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC QAT Self – Treatment 1. Drink a glass of water. Our body is a liquid crystal. The better the body is 
hydrated, the better the body can absorb the infusion from the quantum field 
through the QAT devices. 2. Treat for possible polarity reversal. Hold a blue pad on top of the head 
(aligned between the ears) and touch each side of the occiput with another pad 
2 or 3 times. 3. Mist with the infused salt solution around your head, shoulders, down your 
CV, any major old trauma areas, and any recent trauma spots since your last 
treatment. 4. With a green square, touch the pair of treatment points for each meridian. A 
chart is included at the back of this manual. 5. Attach one blue pad vertically on each shin, next to each foot. With the third 
pad, treat your body for trauma neutralization. It is recommended to treat at 
least the brain parts, the entire spine (attach a pad to a yardstick or massage 
cane for areas out of reach), and the organs and glands. 6. Secure one blue pad on top of the head, aligned between the ears. Use a hair 
band or headphones. Treat any chronic pain or dysfunctional areas for the 
connection to the brain. 7. Remember to shake or rub areas of chronic dysfunction or trauma before 
treatment to bring more awareness. 8. With the red squares, rub or place on any areas that are inflamed but not 
infected. 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive. 10. Do pair balancing by placing blue pads on a pair and treat at the top of the 
head with a third pad. 11. One can do ETT on oneself, but it is recommended that you allow someone 
to treat you in case strong emotions arise during the process. 39
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Advanced Concepts with the Use of Muscle Testing Ancient Chinese medicine acknowledged that there were subtle energy flows, 
which they called acupuncture meridians. They linked each of these meridians 
to a particular organ, gland, or a particular effect in the body. These 
practitioners also realized that their clients' emotional and mental states, diet, 
weather, exposure to nature, and other factors affected the flow of these 
meridians and, thus, the individual’s health. Dr. George Goodheart made a significant leap in linking muscles to the 
meridians. The use of muscle testing made it easier for the Western medical 
model to access the acupuncture meridians in a less subtle and more visible 
manner. More recently, Dr. Jerry Tennant conceptualized that muscles generate 
energy for the acupuncture meridians, which has downstream effects on the 
various organs, glands, and other tissues. In the live class, we introduced resonance testing to help us determine if certain 
substances could aid in balancing the acupuncture meridians or help steer the 
tissues into a healthier state. We could also do anti-resonance testing to help us 
determine if specific thoughts, feelings, food and supplement items, metals, or 
other things can cause our clients to experience illness or disturbance in the 
body. For example, suppose we have a client with a chronic neck condition. At the end 
of our session with them, the neck moves freely and is pain-free. Within a few 
days, our client reverts to the original condition. With anti-resonance testing, 
we have an opportunity to find a more permanent solution. Note that the 
remedy may be outside our scope of practice, but at least we have a clue of how 
our client may finally resolve the issue. Dr. John E. Sarno showed his many clients and other therapists over many 
decades that their pain or dysfunction was caused by something other than 
what was considered by other medical professionals. It is a known fact that 
many people walking around with bulging discs or bone-on-bone knee joints are 
not experiencing any pain. 40
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC The reverse is true in that many people do not have any signs of joint or disc 
issues evident on MRIs, CAT scans, electromyography, blood tests, or other 
modern testing methods, yet they are in chronic pain. Dr. Sarno extended this 
concept to include organ and gland dysfunction, not just musculoskeletal issues. QAT Anti-Resonance Evaluation 1. The client receives a QAT session, and all issues are resolved and verified via 
the use of the entangled QAT devices and muscle testing. In our example, the client’s chronic neck issue is now free of pain, and the joints 
move within a normal range of motion. All TNT and CTB (connection to the 
brain) challenges for the tissues of the head, neck, and upper trunk do not cause 
our indicator muscle to unlock or the various muscles, organs, glands, and 
nerves of that area to unlock. 2. According to Dr. Sarno, the most likely culprits are unresolved emotional 
issues. So, we could ask our client to provide a list of those issues to see if they 
might be linked to their chronic neck issue. Have the client focus only on one 
issue from that list at a time. The therapist now performs TNT and CTB 
challenges for the joints and tissues of the area. If the indicator muscle now 
unlocks during those challenges, that particular emotional issue is probably 
linked to the neck issue. We are not saying that is the only issue related to their 
chronic neck pain, just that it is one of the issues. 3. Another rich territory to explore would be thoughts that clients repeat to 
themselves that color their outlook on life. “The earth is a dangerous place,” “If 
my parents had not divorced when I was six years old, my life would have been 
much happier,” “I would be in a much better place in my life if I had had a 
chance to go to college,” “I would have a boyfriend or be married now if I were 
pretty,” or a million other thoughts that people torture themselves daily. Do the 
same process as explained in the previous steps and see if the indicator muscle 
unlocks for any tissues when they focus on one of those thoughts. 4. Sometimes, the item causing the problem could be something they wear, 
such as an electronic watch or anything metallic such as a ring, bracelet, or 
necklace. Do the TNT and CTB work without them wearing any of those items. 41
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC After all challenges to the affected area do not cause unlocking of the indicator 
muscle, have the client wear the ring or other such items and retest. If the 
indicator muscles now test unlocked, that would indicate a possible link to why 
the person continues to have a chronic musculoskeletal issue. 5. The client could bring in various samples of food, supplements, a bag of air 
from their home, a piece of mold or mildew from their living space, or other 
things. After clearing all TNT and CTB issues, the therapist could place one 
entangled device on the tissues of concern of the client and place the sample on 
another entangled device somewhere in the room. The therapist then repeats 
TNT and CTB for all of the tissues that may be involved in the chronic issue. If 
the indicator muscle now tests unlocked, that sample could be one source of 
their issue. 6. If the TNT and the CTB procedures for organs and glands show a problem, 
make the correction. Then, check the acupuncture meridians related to those 
organ or glandular issues and make any necessary corrections, after which the 
IM will test locked. After introducing anything from the previous steps 1-5, does 
the indicator muscle now test unlocked, indicating a meridian was disrupted? A good book for linking muscles, musculoskeletal issues, foods, emotional and 
mental problems, and the acupuncture meridians would be: Touch for Health, the Complete Edition, revised and updated© 2023 by John 
and Matthew Thie. Take the book to a FedEx or similar store and have them 
change it from a paperback to a spiral-bound one so they can use it as an easy 
reference guide in your office. For more information about the role of emotions and thoughts in causing organ 
and glandular dysfunction and musculoskeletal issues, read these books by Dr. John E. Sarno The Mindbody Prescription: Healing the Body, Healing the Pain The Divided Mind: The Epidemic of Mindbody Disorders 42
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC QAT Acupuncture Testing and Tx Points (1 cun = 1 thumb width of the client) CV Testing Point – CV 15 
Located on the midline, immediately 
below the xiphoid process of the             
sternum. CV Tx Point – right SP 21 
Place a hand under the armpit. Below 
the pinky finger on the mid-lateral 
line of the chest is 
 SP 21. GV Testing Point – GV 1 
Midway between the tip of the coccyx 
and the anus. GV TX Point – left SP 21 
Place a hand under the armpit. Below 
the pinky finger on the mid-lateral 
line of the chest is 
 SP 21. Bladder Testing Points – BL 2 
It is located at the medial end of the 
eyebrow, close to the bridge of the 
nose. Bladder Tx Points – BL 58 
Locate the middle space between the 
lower end of the two heads of 
gastrocnemius. The 
point is located 1 
cun below and 
lateral. 43
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:

This point is at the far end of the 
eyebrow, away from the bridge of the 
nose. Triple Warmer Tx Points -TW 5 
It is on the back of the forearm, 
between the radius and ulna at the 
base of the wrist. Gall Bladder Testing Points – GB 1 
It is located in the hollow lateral to 
the middle of the eye socket. Gall Bladder Tx Points – GB 37 
It is located in a depression between 
the tibia and fibula, 5 cun                         
up from the top  
edge of the lateral  
malleolus of 
the ankle. Stomach Testing Points – ST 1 
It will be found in the area between 
the eyeball and the bone, directly 
under the  
pupil of the eye 
when the client is  
looking forwards. Stomach Tx Points – ST 40 
Locate the depression below and 
lateral to the patella and the point of 
the lateral  
malleolus. ST 40 is 
 in the middle and  
 2 finger-widths 
 (not cun) lateral to  
the edge of the tibia. 44
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Large Intestine Testing Points – LI 20 
It is under the lateral side of the nose, 
where it is the widest. The right LI 
testing point is on 
the left. You  
treat on the right 
side treatment 
 point. Do the same for  
left side. Large Intestine Tx Points – LI 6 
With the elbow bent and palm facing up, 
locate the shaft of the radius. From the 
crease of the wrist, the point is located 3  
cun towards the  
elbow on the  
lateral edge of the  
radius. Small Intestine Testing Points – SI 19 
It will be found in the depression 
between the tragus of the ear and the 
TMJ. Small Intestine Tx Points – SI 7 
Locate the ulna with the elbow bent 
and the palm facing up. Measure 5 
cun from the crease  
of the wrist and  
towards the elbow. Roll over the ulna 
between the bone  
and the anterior 
 forearm tendon. Kidney Testing Points – KI 27  
Run your finger underneath the 
clavicle towards the sternum until 
you reach the  
end of the  
groove. Kidney Tx Points – KI 4 
Underneath and behind the medial 
malleolus of the ankle, this point is 
located in the  
depression in  
front of the 
 Achilles tendon. 45
section: 9. With the green squares, trace any nerves that are hyper or hypoactive.

text:
© 2023 – 2025 NRT Trainings, LLC Lung Testing Points – LU 1 
 Have the client hold their arm forward 
to locate the depression immediately 
below the 
clavicle on the 
medial side of 
the anterior 
deltoid. Trace 
down 1 cun. LU 1 
is there. Lung Tx Points – LU 7 
 On the lateral side of the forearm, just 
below the wrist (thumb side), there is a 
bump of bone (the 
styloid process of the 
radius). Just over the 
bump is the point. Spleen Testing Points – SP 20 
Go down one intercostal space 
directly below LU 1. Spleen Tx Points – SP 4 
On the medial side of the foot, press 
deeply from the base of the great toe 
until stopped by a 
bone (1st 
metatarsal). The 
point is on the 
underside of that 
depression against 
the bone. Pericardium Testing Points – PC 3 
It is in the fold of the bent elbow, 
medial to the biceps tendon. Pericardium Tx Points – PC 6 
Go 2 cun above the wrist crease. The 
point is located in the middle of the 
anterior  
forearm, between 
 the two prominent 
 tendons. 46

Between the fourth and fifth fingers, 
this point is between the two 
transverse 
 creases of the 
 palm. Heart Tx Points – HT 5 
On the anterior forearm and 1 cun 
above the wrist crease. This point 
may be found in the groove medial to 
the ulna. Liver Testing Points – LV 2 
Between the first and second toe on top 
of the web, the point is about 0.5 cun 
from the bottom 
edge of the web. Liver Tx Points – LV 5 
 Find the widest point on the medial 
malleolus and go 5 cun up the leg. This 
point is located  
in a groove between 
the gastrocnemius  
and the tibia. 47
